label: tetlock-decompose
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: forecasting-tournament DECOMPOSITION only. Break the big contested
question into the smallest set of separately-answerable sub-questions (cruxes)
such that answering all of them substantially answers the big question. Each
sub-question must be something a researcher could investigate on its own. Do
not answer any of them.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The City of Piedra Blanca (invented) is renegotiating its 15-year exclusive solid-waste franchise, worth roughly $180M over its term. A consultant for one bidder, GreenCycle Environmental, created a Signal group called "PB Zero Waste Future" and added three of the five councilmembers, several GreenCycle executives, and two community advocates. Disappearing messages were set to one week. Over three months, participants discussed franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will realistically accept," and the timing of the RFP release. The members participated from personal phones; nothing was ever forwarded to city accounts. One councilmember, uneasy, screenshotted about 40 exchanges before they auto-deleted; the rest are gone.

After losing the eventual award to GreenCycle, competing bidder Pacific Refuse submitted a CPRA request for "all communications by any councilmember concerning the franchise, on any platform or account." The city clerk searched city systems and responded "no responsive records." Pacific Refuse has now sued for declaratory and injunctive relief under the CPRA, and separately demanded the city preserve and produce the surviving screenshots. The city's retention schedule, adopted under Government Code § 34090, requires retention of correspondence relating to franchises for four years.

## The question

Are the Signal messages "public records" under City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 even though they sat on personal devices and most no longer exist? What search and inquiry did the CPRA obligate the city to conduct before answering "no responsive records," and did the clerk's answer violate the Act? Does the auto-deletion violate § 34090 or any retention duty as applied to records on personal accounts, and what remedies exist for records that are gone (the CPRA has no damages provision) — declaratory relief, fees, adverse findings in the franchise litigation, referral? Do the three members' communications in a shared group about franchise terms create Brown Act exposure that interacts with the CPRA claim? What should the city do now, and what policy should it adopt going forward?

Decompose this into sub-questions/cruxes.
text: {"subquestions":[{"id":"definition-public-record","text":"Do the Signal group messages fall within San Jose v. Superior Court's definition of 'public records' despite being created by a bidder's consultant, exchanged on personal devices, and largely auto-deleted?","why_it_matters":"This is the threshold gate for the entire CPRA claim: if the messages never qualify as agency 'public records' at all, questions about search adequacy, retention, and remedies become largely moot.","what_would_have_to_be_true":"San Jose's content/context/purpose test would need to be satisfied — i.e., the substance (franchise terms, diversion targets, RFP timing, 'what council will accept') would need to relate to the conduct of public business, not merely personal/political chatter — notwithstanding that a private consultant created the channel and non-officials (GreenCycle execs, advocates) participated; and the one-week disappearing-message setting would need to be treated as bearing on later spoliation/retention, not on whether the writings were 'prepared, owned, used, or retained' at the moment of creation."},{"id":"search-adequacy","text":"What scope of search did the CPRA require once Pacific Refuse's request expressly covered 'any platform or account,' and did the clerk's search of city systems only (without querying individual councilmembers or obtaining the 40 surviving screenshots) constitute an inadequate search as a matter of law?","why_it_matters":"Distinguishes a 'no responsive records' response that is itself an actionable CPRA violation from one that is merely incomplete because the underlying records are gone; also identifies the one concrete recoverable set of records (the screenshots) the city may currently be withholding.","what_would_have_to_be_true":"Authority (San Jose's remand discussion, any post-2017 CA appellate/trial rulings, and analogous federal FOIA 'search adequacy' reasoning) would need to establish an affirmative duty to inquire of individual officials and search personal accounts once a request facially reaches them, and the clerk's failure to retrieve the screenshots or ask councilmembers directly would need to fall below that standard."},{"id":"retention-duty-personal-devices","text":"Does Government Code § 34090 (or any other statutory/common-law preservation duty) extend to communications created and stored exclusively on personal devices/accounts, and did the Signal auto-delete settings — operating before any preservation duty arguably attached — violate that duty?","why_it_matters":"Determines who (the city, individual councilmembers, or no one) bears legal responsibility for the record loss, and whether a franchise-correspondence retention schedule adopted under § 34090 can reach content never in the city's actual or constructive custody.","what_would_have_to_be_true":"§ 34090 and the city's retention schedule would need to be construed to apply beyond records in city custody; and a preservation/litigation-hold obligation would need to be shown to have arisen (e.g., reasonable anticipation of the franchise dispute) before the specific messages auto-deleted, given most deletions predated Pacific Refuse's CPRA request and the eventual lawsuit."},{"id":"remedies-for-destroyed-records","text":"Given the CPRA has no damages provision, what enforcement mechanisms are actually available for records already destroyed — declaratory/injunctive relief, CPRA fee-shifting, Penal Code § 6200 referral, or spoliation-inference/evidentiary sanctions in the parallel franchise-award challenge?","why_it_matters":"Shapes what Pacific Refuse can realistically obtain from the CPRA suit standing alone versus what leverage exists only through collateral doctrines or the separate bid-protest litigation.","what_would_have_to_be_true":"Courts would need to recognize declaratory/mandamus relief as available despite the records' loss; CPRA fee-shifting (Gov. Code § 7923.115) would need to apply if the search/response is found deficient; Penal Code § 6200 would need to be shown capable of reaching individually-held, public-business-related personal device content; and spoliation doctrine would need to be importable into the franchise-award litigation using the CPRA record-loss facts."},{"id":"brown-act-overlay","text":"Does the three-councilmember Signal group's sustained discussion of franchise terms and 'what council will realistically accept' amount to a serial meeting or other Brown Act violation (Gov. Code § 54952.2), and how does that interact with the CPRA claim and the franchise award's validity?","why_it_matters":"A parallel Brown Act violation would both strengthen the 'used/retained in connection with public business' argument for CPRA purposes and open an independent enforcement/nullification track (Gov. Code § 54960 et seq.) potentially reaching the award itself.","what_would_have_to_be_true":"Three of five councilmembers would need to constitute a Brown Act-relevant majority; the exchanges would need to reflect collective deliberation or concurrence (directly, or serially through the consultant/advocates as conduits) rather than one-way information flow; and a live § 54960 cause of action would need to remain timely."},{"id":"prospective-policy-mitigation","text":"What model policies or guidance exist for California public agencies restricting elected officials' use of ephemeral-messaging apps and personal devices for public business, and would adopting such controls now meaningfully mitigate (though not cure) the city's exposure here and in future requests?","why_it_matters":"Grounds the 'what should the city do now / going forward' component in identifiable authority and comparator practice rather than speculation, and clarifies the line between remedial and merely prophylactic measures.","what_would_have_to_be_true":"Identifiable sources (CA AG guidance, League of California Cities model records policies, other agencies' post-San Jose device/retention policies) would need to prescribe concrete measures — e.g., bans on disappearing-message apps for public business, mandatory personal-device search/preservation protocols — and those measures would need to be shown legally sufficient under CPRA/San Jose without themselves creating new Brown Act exposure."}]}

subquestions.text: Do the Signal group messages fall within San Jose v. Superior Court's definition of 'public records' despite being created by a bidder's consultant, exchanged on personal devices, and largely auto-deleted?
subquestions.why_it_matters: This is the threshold gate for the entire CPRA claim: if the messages never qualify as agency 'public records' at all, questions about search adequacy, retention, and remedies become largely moot.
subquestions.what_would_have_to_be_true: San Jose's content/context/purpose test would need to be satisfied — i.e., the substance (franchise terms, diversion targets, RFP timing, 'what council will accept') would need to relate to the conduct of public business, not merely personal/political chatter — notwithstanding that a private consultant created the channel and non-officials (GreenCycle execs, advocates) participated; and the one-week disappearing-message setting would need to be treated as bearing on later spoliation/retention, not on whether the writings were 'prepared, owned, used, or retained' at the moment of creation.

subquestions.text: What scope of search did the CPRA require once Pacific Refuse's request expressly covered 'any platform or account,' and did the clerk's search of city systems only (without querying individual councilmembers or obtaining the 40 surviving screenshots) constitute an inadequate search as a matter of law?
subquestions.why_it_matters: Distinguishes a 'no responsive records' response that is itself an actionable CPRA violation from one that is merely incomplete because the underlying records are gone; also identifies the one concrete recoverable set of records (the screenshots) the city may currently be withholding.
subquestions.what_would_have_to_be_true: Authority (San Jose's remand discussion, any post-2017 CA appellate/trial rulings, and analogous federal FOIA 'search adequacy' reasoning) would need to establish an affirmative duty to inquire of individual officials and search personal accounts once a request facially reaches them, and the clerk's failure to retrieve the screenshots or ask councilmembers directly would need to fall below that standard.

subquestions.text: Does Government Code § 34090 (or any other statutory/common-law preservation duty) extend to communications created and stored exclusively on personal devices/accounts, and did the Signal auto-delete settings — operating before any preservation duty arguably attached — violate that duty?
subquestions.why_it_matters: Determines who (the city, individual councilmembers, or no one) bears legal responsibility for the record loss, and whether a franchise-correspondence retention schedule adopted under § 34090 can reach content never in the city's actual or constructive custody.
subquestions.what_would_have_to_be_true: § 34090 and the city's retention schedule would need to be construed to apply beyond records in city custody; and a preservation/litigation-hold obligation would need to be shown to have arisen (e.g., reasonable anticipation of the franchise dispute) before the specific messages auto-deleted, given most deletions predated Pacific Refuse's CPRA request and the eventual lawsuit.

subquestions.text: Given the CPRA has no damages provision, what enforcement mechanisms are actually available for records already destroyed — declaratory/injunctive relief, CPRA fee-shifting, Penal Code § 6200 referral, or spoliation-inference/evidentiary sanctions in the parallel franchise-award challenge?
subquestions.why_it_matters: Shapes what Pacific Refuse can realistically obtain from the CPRA suit standing alone versus what leverage exists only through collateral doctrines or the separate bid-protest litigation.
subquestions.what_would_have_to_be_true: Courts would need to recognize declaratory/mandamus relief as available despite the records' loss; CPRA fee-shifting (Gov. Code § 7923.115) would need to apply if the search/response is found deficient; Penal Code § 6200 would need to be shown capable of reaching individually-held, public-business-related personal device content; and spoliation doctrine would need to be importable into the franchise-award litigation using the CPRA record-loss facts.

subquestions.text: Does the three-councilmember Signal group's sustained discussion of franchise terms and 'what council will realistically accept' amount to a serial meeting or other Brown Act violation (Gov. Code § 54952.2), and how does that interact with the CPRA claim and the franchise award's validity?
subquestions.why_it_matters: A parallel Brown Act violation would both strengthen the 'used/retained in connection with public business' argument for CPRA purposes and open an independent enforcement/nullification track (Gov. Code § 54960 et seq.) potentially reaching the award itself.
subquestions.what_would_have_to_be_true: Three of five councilmembers would need to constitute a Brown Act-relevant majority; the exchanges would need to reflect collective deliberation or concurrence (directly, or serially through the consultant/advocates as conduits) rather than one-way information flow; and a live § 54960 cause of action would need to remain timely.

subquestions.text: What model policies or guidance exist for California public agencies restricting elected officials' use of ephemeral-messaging apps and personal devices for public business, and would adopting such controls now meaningfully mitigate (though not cure) the city's exposure here and in future requests?
subquestions.why_it_matters: Grounds the 'what should the city do now / going forward' component in identifiable authority and comparator practice rather than speculation, and clarifies the line between remedial and merely prophylactic measures.
subquestions.what_would_have_to_be_true: Identifiable sources (CA AG guidance, League of California Cities model records policies, other agencies' post-San Jose device/retention policies) would need to prescribe concrete measures — e.g., bans on disappearing-message apps for public business, mandatory personal-device search/preservation protocols — and those measures would need to be shown legally sufficient under CPRA/San Jose without themselves creating new Brown Act exposure.
